summary: Julia describes pairing with Will Byrd to implement binary search trees using core.logic in Clojure. The key revelation is that logic programming lets you run functions 'backwards' — instead of just checking if a tree contains a value, you can ask for trees that contain a given value. She shows the code for a containso function and demonstrates both forward and backward queries, generating 10 different binary trees that all contain the number 3.
key_points: Logic programming with core.logic allows bidirectional queries — you can check membership or generate structures that satisfy constraints
Running a logic program 'backwards' to generate valid data structures from constraints is the compelling feature of relational programming
The code is remarkably concise — a few lines of core.logic define a relational binary search tree
money_quotes: My favorite thing about logic programming is that all the functions end with 'o'.
But the really fun part is that you can run it "backwards" and ask for 10 trees that contain 3.
If you inspect carefully, you will see that those are all binary trees and they all contain 3!
key_insight: Logic programming's power lies in running programs backwards — instead of just verifying properties, you can generate all structures that satisfy a given constraint.
